name: The death of John the Baptist
notes: The threefold synoptic account of Herod hearing of the fame of Jesus and the beheading of John the Baptist. Matthew and Mark share a full core: Herod hears of Jesus and supposes John is risen, the prior imprisonment for Herodias' sake (his brother Philip's wife), John's word that it was not lawful, the birthday feast, the dancing daughter, the rash oath, the head asked in a charger, the king sorry yet bound by his oath, the beheading in the prison, and the disciples taking up the body. Luke's account is brief and is linked only at the genuine overlap, Herod's perplexity and his word 'John have I beheaded', with the full backstory of the feast left unstated in Luke. The accounts give complementary vantages: Matthew notes Herod feared the multitude that counted John a prophet, while Mark adds that Herod himself feared John as a just and holy man and heard him gladly, two angles on the one king's divided heart.
